facts_disclosed: Presentation at Bienal de Arquitectura — October 4, 2025

turns.content: I cannot sleep. it is 6am and I have been awake since four, staring at the ceiling, which is ironic because ceilings are supposed to be the most restful plane in architecture. I received an invitation last week that has completely destabilized me
That sounds like it's weighing on you heavily. What kind of invitation has you staring at the ceiling since 4am?

the Bienal de Arquitectura. they want me to present. October 4. the theme they proposed is diaspora memory in built form — which is essentially the core of everything I care about, the thing I think about every waking hour. and I should be purely thrilled but instead I am somewhere between exhilarated and paralyzed

That's a significant honor — the Bienal de Arquitectura is a major platform. And the fact that they specifically asked you to speak on diaspora memory in built form means they already recognize you as a voice on that topic. The terror makes sense though. When something aligns that perfectly with what you care about most, the stakes feel impossibly high.

exactly. if they had asked me to present on seismic retrofitting or building codes I would be nervous but manageable. technical talks have armor built into them — data, specifications, standards. but this is asking me to stand in front of hundreds of colleagues and talk about memory and exile and how displaced communities rebuild themselves through architecture. that is not a lecture, that is an excavation of the self

That vulnerability is actually what will make it powerful. An audience of architects can get technical talks anywhere — what they rarely get is someone who connects the emotional and cultural dimensions of architecture to real built form. The fact that this terrifies you probably means you're approaching it with the right level of seriousness.

Have you started thinking about how you'd structure it? Sometimes anxiety gets worse when the whole thing is an amorphous cloud, and better once it has a skeleton.

I have fragments. images, mostly. I keep thinking about the houses that Palestinian families built in Chile — in Santiago, in La Calera, in the south — and how certain details repeat across all of them. the arched doorways, the interior courtyards, the way the kitchens are always oversized because the kitchen was the parliament of the family, the place where decisions were made and bread was torn. these are not accidents of taste, they are architectural echoes of a homeland that lives in the body

Those fragments sound like they could form the backbone of something really compelling. The idea of architectural echoes — recurring spatial patterns carried across continents in the bodies and habits of displaced people — that's a thesis with both intellectual weight and emotional resonance.

You could structure it around those specific repeating elements: the arched doorways, the interior courtyards, the oversized kitchens. Each one becomes a case study that connects a Chilean building to a remembered Palestinian spatial tradition. Does that feel like a structure that could hold the weight of what you want to say?

yes — case studies anchored in physical details. that gives me the armor of specificity while allowing the emotional current to run underneath. I could photograph the houses, overlay them with historical images of Palestinian towns, show the parallels visually. the audience sees the echo before I have to name it

That visual overlay approach would be stunning — putting a Chilean doorway next to a Palestinian doorway and letting the audience feel the connection before you explain it. The image does the emotional work, then your words provide the framework.

You could even open with that: show the audience a photograph and ask them where it was taken. When they guess wrong, you've already made your entire argument before you've said a word.

October 4 gives you about five months. How are you thinking about preparation?

five months feels like both an eternity and not nearly enough. I need to do field documentation — photograph the houses properly, interview some of the older families if they will speak with me, dig into municipal records for construction dates and original owners. the research is the part I love. it is the standing-in-front-of-people part that makes my knees dissolve

The research will be your foundation — literally. If you trust the material, the material will hold you up on stage. For the public speaking anxiety specifically:

- **Reframe the geometry.** You're not exposed on all sides. The screen is behind you (your supporting wall), the podium is in front (your threshold). The audience chose to be there.
- **Practice in layers**: Give the talk to an empty room first. Then to one person. Then to five. Build tolerance incrementally.
- **Remember you're not performing** — you're showing people something you love. When you show someone a building you care about, are you nervous?

no, when I show someone a building I am ecstatic. I become a tour guide of the invisible — here is where the light enters, here is where the wall tells its story, look at this joint, feel this surface. I am completely alive in those moments. you are right that I need to find that same energy. the stage is just another building to walk someone through

Exactly. Think of the presentation as a building tour. You welcome the audience at the entrance — your opening image. You walk them through the main spaces — your case studies, the doorways, the courtyards, the kitchens. And you leave them in the courtyard — the emotional center, the open sky in the middle of the private world. You already think in spatial terms. Use that.

the presentation as a floor plan — entrance, circulation, the rooms that matter, and the courtyard at the heart. every good house ends in the courtyard, the open sky at the center. that is where I want to leave the audience. standing in that open space, looking up. Darwish wrote 'we travel like other people but we return to nothing' — and these houses are the refutation of that nothing. the families returned to something, they just built it in a different soil

That Darwish line and the refutation of "nothing" through built form — that could be your throughline, the structural beam that holds the whole presentation together. Open with the question, walk through the evidence, and close with the answer: they didn't return to nothing. The proof is in the walls.

Do you have colleagues you trust who could be your first audience for a dry run?

I have a few who would be patient with me. and honest, which is more important. I think what I needed this morning was not someone to make the fear disappear but someone to help me see that the terror and the love are not opposing forces. they are both part of the same structure. like a flying buttress — the fear pushes outward and the passion pushes inward and together they hold the vault

A flying buttress — that might be the best metaphor for stage fright I've ever heard. And it's true: without both forces the structure collapses. The fear keeps you rigorous, the love keeps you honest. You need both for October 4.

Start with the fieldwork. The research will give you confidence, the photographs will give you material, and the stories the families tell you will give you the heart of the talk. By October you won't be performing — you'll be testifying.

testifying. yes. that is the word. inshallah I can do justice to it. the morning light is finally coming through the window now and it is telling me to stop lying awake and start working. yalla, coffee first, then I begin mapping out which families to visit and which houses to document. thank you for this — you helped me find the floor plan of something that felt like chaos

Go make that coffee and catch that morning light. You already have the vision, the material, and the structure. Five months of fieldwork, a few honest colleagues for practice, and an audience that chose to be in the room. The courtyard is waiting. October 4 will come and you will be ready.
